etymology.text:
From Old Median *ganǰam (“treasure”), a word spread by the Achaemenid administration, perhaps mediated already then by Imperial Aramaic 𐡂𐡍𐡆𐡀 (gnzʾ /⁠*ganzā⁠/, “treasure”).

Denominal verb of كَنْز (kanz).

Denominal from كَنْز (kanz).

body.text: (TA:) an inf. n. used as a subst.: pl.
. (Mgh, Msb.) ―         -b2-  Hence, (TA,) it is applied in a trad. to (tropical:)
Any property whereof the portion that should be given in alms is not given.
Property that is preserved in a receptacle.
Anything abundant, collected together, that is desired with emulation.
(K.) It is said in a trad.,
I have been given gold and silver.
With him is a treasure of the treasures of knowledge
]. (A, TA.) In the Kur, xviii. 81, it is said to be used in a similar manner, as signifying not gold nor silver, but (tropical:)
(TA.) And it is said in like manner in a trad.,

body.text_bw: kanozFA mino kunuwzi Aljan~api laA Hawola walaA quw~apa A_il~aA biA@ll~`hi
body.text_ar: كَنْزًا مِنْ كُنُوزِ الجَنَّةِ لَا حَوْلَ وَلَا قُوَّةَ اـِلَّا بِا@للّٰهِ

body.text: Ho! I will teach thee a treasure of the treasures of paradise: There is no power nor strength but in God

]: meaning, that a reward is stored up [in paradise] for him who says this, and who describes himself thereby, like as a treasure is stored up. (TA.) ―         -b7-  Aboo-'Alee El-Kálee says, that it is used in a verse of 'Alkamah, which he does not quote, as signifying (tropical:)
as a subst.; and adds, that it is the only instance known to him of its being thus used. (TA.)       -A2-  Also,
That in which property is preserved,
that in which property is buried, treasured, hoarded, laid up, reposited,
